tamil: டின் தாய் ஃபங் அன்னாசிப் பழ கேக்

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Taiwanese pineapple cake (feng li su). The closest defensible parent is 'pastry food product' (FOODON_03315091), which covers butter-based shortcrust pastry items with a fruit filling.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LC (Pastries and cakes) is the closest defensible parent category for feng li su. No specific code exists for Taiwanese pineapple cake in the FoodEx2 hierarchy.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept exists for Taiwanese pineapple cake or feng li su. SNOMED CT does not provide sufficient granularity for this confection category.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard Din Tai Fung pineapple cake weighs approximately 40 g; a box of 10 contains approximately 400 g total.

nutrient_source.title: Pineapple cake (feng li su) — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy / Branded Foods composite; cross-referenced with Taiwan TFDA nutrition labelling data for comparable commercial pineapple cakes
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central; Taiwan Food and Drug Administration (TFDA) nutrition labelling reference
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact USDA FoodData Central entry exists for Din Tai Fung pineapple cake. Values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy shortbread/butter cookie entries (NDB 18158, 18159) combined with published Taiwan TFDA nutrition label data for commercial feng li su products (approximately 450–480 kcal/100 g, 5–6 g protein, 18–22 g fat, 65–70 g carbohydrate). The composite estimate reflects a buttery shortcrust shell (~60% by weight) with a sweetened pineapple jam filling (~40% by weight). Values are rounded to one decimal place and represent a best-estimate for a generic commercial feng li su; actual Din Tai Fung product values may differ slightly.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of shortbread and butter biscuit analogues; Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC — International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Taiwanese pineapple cake (feng li su). GI is estimated at 65 (medium) by analogy with butter shortbread biscuits and filled fruit pastries as listed in the University of Sydney GI Database and the International Tables of GI and GL Values (Atkinson et al., 2008, Diabetes Care 31(12):2281-2283). The buttery shortcrust shell has a GI of approximately 55–65; the high-sugar pineapple jam filling raises the blended GI toward the medium range. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (65 × 26.4) / 100 = 17.2 (serving = 40 g, carbohydrate = 66.0 × 0.40 = 26.4 g).

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content and high energy density
hypertension.guidance: Each pineapple cake (40 g) contributes approximately 64 mg sodium; individuals with hypertension should limit portion size and account for cumulative sodium from other dietary sources, as the box of 10 provides approximately 640 mg sodium in total.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High sugar and refined carbohydrate content with medium glycemic index
type2_diabetes.guidance: Each pineapple cake contains approximately 26 g of carbohydrate and 15 g of sugar, with a medium GI of 65 and a glycemic load of approximately 17 per piece; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit consumption to one piece per occasion and pair with a low-GI food to blunt postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High saturated fat, trans fat, and added sugar content
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Each pineapple cake provides approximately 4.8 g of saturated fat and 0.08 g of trans fat; individuals with cardiovascular disease should restrict consumption given the combined atherogenic risk from saturated fat, trans fat, and the high added sugar load of approximately 15 g per piece.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Din Tai Fung Pineapple Cake (鳳梨酥, feng li su) is a classic Taiwanese pastry confection sold by the internationally acclaimed Din Tai Fung restaurant group. Each cake consists of a crumbly, buttery shortcrust shell made from wheat flour, butter, sugar, and egg, encasing a sweet-tangy pineapple jam filling made from natural Taiwanese pineapples and maltose. The pastry is baked until golden and has a melt-in-the-mouth texture with a rich, buttery crumble and a bright, fruity centre. Din Tai Fung markets these cakes as handmade with no artificial preservatives, and they are sold in a signature box of 10 pieces (approximately 40 g each). Nutritionally, each piece provides approximately 184 kcal, 26 g carbohydrate, 8 g fat, and 15 g sugar, making it a high-energy, high-sugar confection. The medium glycemic index (estimated ~65) and high glycemic load (~17 per piece) make it a food of concern for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or cardiovascular disease. The saturated fat content (~4.8 g per piece) is clinically relevant for cardiovascular risk management. Pineapple cake is a popular gift item during Lunar New Year and other festive occasions in Taiwan, Singapore, and across the Chinese diaspora. It is widely available in Singapore through Din Tai Fung outlets and delivery platforms.

confidence_reason: No exact nutritional entry for Din Tai Fung pineapple cake exists in any approved primary source (HPB Singapore Foodo, USDA FoodData Central, ICMR-NIN, or FAO/INFOODS SEA). Values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy butter shortbread/cookie entries cross-referenced with Taiwan TFDA nutrition labelling data for comparable commercial feng li su products. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database using butter shortbread biscuit data. Confidence is therefore medium.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy shortbread/butter cookie entries (NDB 18158, 18159) and Taiwan TFDA nutrition labelling data for commercial feng li su; actual Din Tai Fung product values may differ. 2. GI of 65 is an analogous estimate based on butter shortbread biscuits from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for feng li su is available in approved sources. 3. vitamin_b6_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the reference source composite. 4. vitamin_b12_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the reference source composite. 5. folate_mcg_dfe is -1 as it is not reported in the reference source composite. 6. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the reference source composite. 7. vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the reference source composite. 8. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the reference source composite. 9. Halal status is not confirmed for Din Tai Fung Singapore pineapple cakes; is_halal is set to false pending official certification verification. 10. The food_name has been normalised to the canonical English form 'Din Tai Fung Pineapple Cake'; the box-of-10 commercial descriptor is captured in aliases. 11. Multilingual names follow the Singaporean dish rule (English, Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) given the food is sold in Singapore, with Chinese names reflecting the Taiwanese origin of the dish.
